title: Australian Privacy Principles compliance monitoring
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the AU Privacy Act programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with AU Privacy Act control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: AU Privacy Act APP 1 — UC provides continuous Splunk telemetry evidence for this regulatory requirement, uplifted from contributing to partial after review confirmed the SPL captures relevant monitoring signals.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that AU Privacy Act APP 1 (Open and transparent management of personal info) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.31.3: Australian Privacy Principles compliance monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.31.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: AU Privacy Act §26WK — UC provides continuous Splunk telemetry evidence for this regulatory requirement, uplifted from contributing to partial after review confirmed the SPL captures relevant monitoring signals.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that AU Privacy Act §26WK (NDB — notifiable data breach) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.31.3: Australian Privacy Principles compliance monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.31.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Australian Privacy Principles compliance monitoring' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper AU Privacy Act control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the AU Privacy Act control objective.

dataSources: Windows Security Event Logs, endpoint protection logs, cloud audit trails, vulnerability scan outputs, email security logs, network infrastructure logs, identity management systems, incident response records
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for CrowdStrike FDR (5082), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621) (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=app sourcetype="crm:audit" region="AU" earliest=-30d
| stats count by object, Operation
| search Operation="Export" AND NOT match(object,"(?i)de_identified")
description: Evidences Privacy Act 1988 (Cth); NDB obligations for australian privacy principles compliance monitoring using endpoint, identity, and cloud telemetry.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for AU Privacy Act compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes app and sourcetypes crm:audit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Ingest CrowdStrike policy posture via TA 5082 and map hosts to NDB and NZISM tiers. (2) Tune alerts with ACSD Essential Eight and APRA-aligned severities. (3) Coordinate privacy and security ownership for dual-reporting workflows. (4) Monthly export for privacy officer and prudential working groups. Indexes required: `index=app`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=crm:audit`.
visualization: Single value (% compliant hosts), Bar chart (fails by control), Table (worst hosts). Key fields: `Operation`, `object`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes by Processes.process_name, Processes.user, Processes.dest | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the CIM data model referenced in cimSpl.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `app` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `APP 1`. The control objective is: "Evidence that AU Privacy Act APP 1 (Open and transparent management of personal info) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.31.3: Australian Privacy Principles compliance monitoring." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `app` under `crm:audit`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Ingest CrowdStrike policy posture via TA 5082 and map hosts to NDB and NZISM tiers. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 2:** Tune alerts with ACSD Essential Eight and APRA-aligned severities. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

• **Hint 3:** Coordinate privacy and security ownership for dual-reporting workflows. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 4:** Monthly export for privacy officer and prudential working groups. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `count()` across the last 30 days, grouped by `object, Operation`. For "Australian Privacy Principles compliance monitoring," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the AU Privacy Act control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. The aggregation approach is chosen to produce one result row per analytical unit, which is the typical requirement for compliance dashboarding and report generation.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `object, Operation` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(object) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Endpoint` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes by Processes.process_name, Processes.user, Processes.dest | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Endpoint` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.31.3: Australian Privacy Principles compliance monitoring]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.31.3,reg=AU_Privacy_Act
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=app sourcetype=crm:audit earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `Operation`, `object`. Run:

```spl
index=app sourcetype=crm:audit earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table Operation, object
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.31.3: AU Privacy Act evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Single-value KPI (`% compliant hosts`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'AU Privacy Act current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 2 — Distribution (`fails by control`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 3 — Drill-through table (`worst hosts`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**AU Privacy Act-specific failure modes:**

• APP (Australian Privacy Principles) scope: APPs define 13 principles covering collection, use, disclosure. Detections on privacy controls must cite specific APP number — 'privacy violation' alone is not actionable.

• Notifiable Data Breach scheme: 30-day OAIC notification from awareness of eligible data breach. Detections measuring breach SLA start from first eligible-breach determination, not raw incident detection.

• Serious harm threshold: NDB scheme only triggers on 'likely to result in serious harm' breaches. Detections must include harm likelihood scoring; routine access anomalies don't automatically meet the threshold.

• Privacy Act 2024 reforms: major reforms introduce mandatory Privacy Impact Assessments and direct right of action. Detections must anticipate the revised APPs; UCs authored pre-reform need reconciliation.
grandmaExplanation: We help you show security and resilience evidence that Australian and New Zealand prudential standards ask for, in one place.
